name: दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोग
teaching: Two inheritances run through every human heart: one of fearlessness, charity, and harmlessness; one of hypocrisy, arrogance, and the creed that the world is loveless and false, with desire its only law. Krishna names desire, anger, and greed as the threefold gate of self-destruction — and scripture as the lamp for telling the two inheritances apart when they speak in your own voice. Two inheritances contend in every heart. The dark one is anatomized without flinching — its creed, its monologue, its three doors — and the bright one begins where fear ends. The chapter closes by handing the seeker a measure beyond appetite.
questions: Which 'demonic' trait do you recognise not in others but in yourself, even faintly?
Desire, anger, greed — which gate stands open widest in your life right now?
When your impulses and your principles conflict, what do you actually consult?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अभयं सत्त्वसंशुद्धिर्ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थितिः ।
दानं दमश्च यज्ञश्च स्वाध्यायस्तप आर्जवम्॥
verses.transliteration:
abhayaṃ sattvasaṃśuddhirjñānayogavyavasthitiḥ
dānaṃ damaśca yajñaśca svādhyāyastapa ārjavam
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: fearlessness, purity of being, steadfastness in knowledge and yoga, charity, restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness —
verses.translation: Fearlessness, purity of heart, steadfastness in knowledge and Yoga, almsgiving, control of the senses, sacrifice, study of scriptures, austerity and straightforwardness.
verses.explanation: The inventory of the divine endowment begins, and its first word is the foundation of all the rest: abhayam, fearlessness. Not bravado — the deep unafraidness of one who has nothing to protect because nothing essential can be lost. From that root grow purity, steadiness, generosity, discipline: the whole list is what a heart does when fear stops governing it. Abhayam sattva-samshuddhih — the divine inventory opens with fearlessness, and the commentaries insist on the order: every subsequent virtue is what a heart does once fear stops governing it. Jnaneshwar glosses abhaya as the absence of the primal flinch — the settled knowledge that nothing essential can be taken. Twenty-six qualities follow; the tradition reads them as one quality, fearlessness, conjugated through a life.
verses.connection: Opens the chapter's ledger of two natures — the daivi sampad listed first (16.1–3).
verses.reflection: Begin at the list's beginning today: practice one hour of abhayam — acting with nothing to protect.
verses.background: Without a question asked, Krishna inventories two inheritances contending in every heart. First the divine endowment, twenty-six qualities beginning with fearlessness and purity of being — charity, restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness, harmlessness, truth, freedom from anger, tranquility, compassion toward beings — born for liberation. Then the demonic: ostentation, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, ignorance. Grieve not, he adds — you are born to the divine.

verses.devanagari:
अहिंसा सत्यमक्रोधस्त्यागः शान्तिरपैशुनम् ।
दया भूतेष्वलोलुप्त्वं मार्दवं ह्रीरचापलम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ahiṃsā satyamakrodhastyāgaḥ śāntirapaiśunam
dayā bhūteṣvaloluptvaṃ mārdavaṃ hrīracāpalam
verses.essence: — harmlessness, truth, freedom from anger, renunciation, tranquility, absence of slander, compassion toward beings, freedom from greed, gentleness, modesty, absence of fickleness —
verses.translation: Harmlessness, truth, absence of anger, renunciation, peacefulness, absence of crookedness, compassion towards beings, non-covetousness, gentleness, modesty, absence of fickleness.
verses.explanation: The list's middle section reads like a description of how it feels to be near such a person: no cutting words behind backs (apaiśunam), tenderness toward creatures (dayā bhūteṣu), mārdavam — softness — and hrī, the capacity to blush, conscience worn on the skin. The divine endowment is mostly composed of harms not done and gentleness actively present.
verses.connection: The daivi inventory continues — virtues of relation, the social face of fearlessness.
verses.reflection: Choose three from the middle of the list today: no slander, tenderness to creatures, the capacity to blush.
verses.background: Without a question asked, Krishna inventories two inheritances contending in every heart. First the divine endowment, twenty-six qualities beginning with fearlessness and purity of being — charity, restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness, harmlessness, truth, freedom from anger, tranquility, compassion toward beings — born for liberation. Then the demonic: ostentation, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, ignorance. Grieve not, he adds — you are born to the divine.

verses.devanagari:
तेजः क्षमा धृतिः शौचमद्रोहो नातिमानिता ।
भवन्ति सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातस्य भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
tejaḥ kṣamā dhṛtiḥ śaucamadroho nātimānitā
bhavanti sampadaṃ daivīmabhijātasya bhārata
verses.essence: — vigor, forgiveness, fortitude, purity, absence of malice, absence of arrogance: these belong to one born to the divine endowment, Bharata.
verses.translation: Vigour, forgiveness, fortitude, purity, absence of hatred, absence of pride these belong to the one born for a divine state, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: absence of malice, absence of excessive pride
these belong to the endowment divine
of one born to it, O Bharata

verses.explanation: Twenty-six qualities close with a balanced pair: tejas, fire — the divine nature is not meek — held beside kṣamā, the forgiveness that keeps fire from becoming cruelty. Abhijātasya, 'of one born to it': temperament deals the opening hand. But the Gita's whole arc insists the hand can be replayed — these are listed precisely so they can be cultivated.
verses.connection: Completes the divine portrait; 16.4 will need only one verse for its opposite.
verses.reflection: Hold fire and forgiveness together once today — tejas with kshama, strength that refuses cruelty.
verses.background: Without a question asked, Krishna inventories two inheritances contending in every heart. First the divine endowment, twenty-six qualities beginning with fearlessness and purity of being — charity, restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness, harmlessness, truth, freedom from anger, tranquility, compassion toward beings — born for liberation. Then the demonic: ostentation, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, ignorance. Grieve not, he adds — you are born to the divine.

verses.devanagari:
दम्भो दर्पोऽभिमानश्च क्रोधः पारुष्यमेव च ।
अज्ञानं चाभिजातस्य पार्थ सम्पदमासुरीम्॥
verses.transliteration:
dambho darpo'bhimānaśca krodhaḥ pāruṣyameva ca
ajñānaṃ cābhijātasya pārtha sampadamāsurīm
verses.essence: Hypocrisy, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, and ignorance: these, Partha, belong to one born to the demonic endowment.
verses.translation: Hypocrisy, arrogance and self-conceit, anger and also harshness and ignorance, belong to one who is born for a demoniacal state, O Partha (Arjuna).

anvaya.meaning: O Partha, to the endowment demonic

verses.explanation: Against twenty-six virtues, six vices — the asymmetry is the comment: darkness is structurally simpler than light. The six chain naturally: dambha, the performance of virtue not possessed; darpa and abhimāna, the swollen self; krodha when the world disagrees; pāruṣya, the voice gone hard; and beneath all five, ajñāna — not knowing what one is. Note what is absent: no mention of horns or hells. The demonic is a psychology, available anywhere.
verses.connection: The counter-ledger, brutally brief — and 16.5 immediately reassures the listener.
verses.reflection: Run the six-point check honestly: where does performance, swagger, or hardness of voice live in me?
verses.background: Without a question asked, Krishna inventories two inheritances contending in every heart. First the divine endowment, twenty-six qualities beginning with fearlessness and purity of being — charity, restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness, harmlessness, truth, freedom from anger, tranquility, compassion toward beings — born for liberation. Then the demonic: ostentation, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, ignorance. Grieve not, he adds — you are born to the divine.

verses.devanagari:
दैवी सम्पद्विमोक्षाय निबन्धायासुरी मता ।
मा शुचः सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातोऽसि पाण्डव॥
verses.transliteration:
daivī sampadvimokṣāya nibandhāyāsurī matā
mā śucaḥ sampadaṃ daivīmabhijāto'si pāṇḍava
verses.essence: The divine endowment leads to liberation, the demonic to bondage, it is held. Grieve not, Pandava — you are born to the divine endowment.
verses.translation: The divine nature is deemed conducive to liberation, and the demoniacal to bondage. Grieve not, O Arjuna, thou art born with divine endowments.

anvaya.meaning: the divine endowment is for liberation
the demonic, for bondage — so it is held
grieve not — to the endowment divine

verses.explanation: The stakes named — freedom or bondage — and then the teaching pauses for tenderness: mā śucaḥ, do not grieve. Krishna has watched Arjuna silently auditing himself against both lists, and answers the unspoken dread directly: *you are of the divine*. A teacher who diagnoses the demonic in the world while reassuring the anxious student in front of him — the chapter's severity is framed, start to finish, by this kindness.
verses.connection: The pastoral hinge: Arjuna cleared by name before the dark portrait deepens.
verses.reflection: Hear the reassurance addressed to you as well: grieve not — the divine endowment is your birth side.
verses.background: Without a question asked, Krishna inventories two inheritances contending in every heart. First the divine endowment, twenty-six qualities beginning with fearlessness and purity of being — charity, restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness, harmlessness, truth, freedom from anger, tranquility, compassion toward beings — born for liberation. Then the demonic: ostentation, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, ignorance. Grieve not, he adds — you are born to the divine.

verses.devanagari:
द्वौ भूतसर्गौ लोकेऽस्मिन्दैव आसुर एव च ।
दैवो विस्तरशः प्रोक्त आसुरं पार्थ मे शृणु॥
verses.transliteration:
dvau bhūtasargau loke'smindaiva āsura eva ca
daivo vistaraśaḥ prokta āsuraṃ pārtha me śṛṇu
verses.essence: Two orders of beings are created in this world: the divine and the demonic. The divine has been described at length; hear from me, Partha, of the demonic.
verses.translation: There are two types of beings in this world, the divine and the demoniacal; the divine has been described at length; hear from Me, O Arjuna, of the demoniacal.

anvaya.meaning: two creations of beings in this world
the divine has been told at length
the demonic, O Partha, hear from me

verses.explanation: The transition, with its quiet arithmetic: the divine took three verses and sixteen chapters of teaching; the demonic will now receive a full anatomy — because what must be guarded against must be understood. What follows (16.7–20) is scripture's most unflinching psychological portrait: not of monsters, but of a worldview, drawn from the inside.
verses.connection: Announces the long descent: the asuric mind examined from its premises to its end.
verses.reflection: Study the dark portrait today as a mirror-check, not a verdict on others.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च जना न विदुरासुराः ।
न शौचं नापि चाचारो न सत्यं तेषु विद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca janā na vidurāsurāḥ
na śaucaṃ nāpi cācāro na satyaṃ teṣu vidyate
verses.essence: The demonic do not know the way of action nor the way of restraint. Neither purity, nor right conduct, nor truth is found in them.
verses.translation: The demoniacal know not what to do and what to refrain from; neither purity, nor right conduct nor truth is found in them.

anvaya.word: न शौचं न अपि च आचारः

anvaya.meaning: nor truth is found in them

verses.explanation: The diagnosis begins at the compass: pravṛtti and nivṛtti — when to act, when to refrain — the basic moral directionality every tradition teaches, simply missing. Not defied; *unknown*. And the third absence is the deepest: na satyam — truth not present in them, first as honesty, finally as any felt contact with the real. The portrait will now show what a life builds on that vacancy.
verses.connection: The asuric anatomy opens at its root: no inner north, no anchoring truth.
verses.reflection: Verify your compass first thing today: do I actually know, right now, when to act and when to refrain?
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
असत्यमप्रतिष्ठं ते जगदाहुरनीश्वरम् ।
अपरस्परसम्भूतं किमन्यत्कामहैतुकम्॥
verses.transliteration:
asatyamapratiṣṭhaṃ te jagadāhuranīśvaram
aparasparasambhūtaṃ kimanyatkāmahaitukam
verses.essence: They say the world is without truth, without foundation, without God — born of mutual union, with desire for its cause; what else?
verses.translation: They say, "This universe is without truth, without (moral) basis, without a God, brought about by mutual union, with lust for its cause; what else?"

anvaya.meaning: say the world is, 'without a Lord'

verses.explanation: The asuric creed, stated fairly before it is judged: no truth beneath appearances, no moral foundation, no Lord; the cosmos a chain of couplings driven by appetite — kim anyat, *what else could there be?* The shrug at the end is the signature. The Gita's objection is not to inquiry but to where this premise leads when lived: the next verses trace a worldview becoming a wake of destruction.
verses.connection: The philosophical seed (16.8) whose human harvest 16.9–20 will follow step by step.
verses.schools_note: The asuric creed — 'without truth, without basis, without God, born of desire' — was identified by classical commentators with the Lokayata (Charvaka) materialists, and by some with hedonist readings of any school. Modern readers note the Gita refutes it not by argument here but by biography: 16.9-20 simply runs the worldview forward to its lived conclusion. The method is the message: test premises by the lives they build.
verses.reflection: Notice where 'what else could there be?' has crept into your worldview — and reopen one window.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
एतां दृष्टिमवष्टभ्य नष्टात्मानोऽल्पबुद्धयः ।
प्रभवन्त्युग्रकर्माणः क्षयाय जगतोऽहिताः॥
verses.transliteration:
etāṃ dṛṣṭimavaṣṭabhya naṣṭātmāno'lpabuddhayaḥ
prabhavantyugrakarmāṇaḥ kṣayāya jagato'hitāḥ
verses.essence: Holding fast to this view, these lost souls of small understanding rise up as enemies of the world, of cruel deeds, for its destruction.
verses.translation: Holding this view, these ruined souls of small intellect and fierce deeds, come forth as the enemies of the world for its destruction.

anvaya.meaning: they rise up, of fierce deeds
for the ruin of the world, its enemies

verses.explanation: The chain from creed to deed: avaṣṭabhya — *clinging* to the view, leaning whole weight on it. If nothing is sacred and desire is the only engine, then ugra-karmāṇaḥ, ruthless action, becomes simply rational. Naṣṭātmānaḥ, the lost selves: the verse's pity inside its alarm — the world's destroyers are first of all people who have misplaced themselves.
verses.connection: 16.8's philosophy goes operational: ideas, the Gita insists, have body counts.
verses.reflection: Trace one idea you hold to its lived consequences today — philosophies have body counts.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
काममाश्रित्य दुष्पूरं दम्भमानमदान्विताः ।
मोहाद्गृहीत्वासद्ग्राहान्प्रवर्तन्तेऽशुचिव्रताः॥
verses.transliteration:
kāmamāśritya duṣpūraṃ dambhamānamadānvitāḥ
mohādgṛhītvāsadgrāhānpravartante'śucivratāḥ
verses.essence: Surrendered to insatiable desire, full of hypocrisy, pride and intoxication, holding false notions through delusion, they act with impure resolves.
verses.translation: Filled with insatiable desires, full of hypocrisy, pride and arrogance, holding evil ideas through delusion, they work with impure resolves.
verses.explanation: The engine room: kāmam duṣpūram — desire that *cannot* be filled, a vessel with no bottom, taken as one's refuge (āśritya, the same word elsewhere used for taking refuge in God). The substitution is the whole tragedy: appetite installed in the throne meant for the eternal. Around it gather hypocrisy, pride, and mada, intoxication — drunk on self.
verses.connection: Names the asuric center of gravity: bottomless craving enthroned as refuge.
verses.reflection: Find the bottomless vessel you keep refilling — and stop pouring for one day.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
चिन्तामपरिमेयां च प्रलयान्तामुपाश्रिताः ।
कामोपभोगपरमा एतावदिति निश्चिताः॥
verses.transliteration:
cintāmaparimeyāṃ ca pralayāntāmupāśritāḥ
kāmopabhogaparamā etāvaditi niścitāḥ
verses.essence: Beset by immeasurable cares ending only with death, holding the enjoyment of desire as the highest aim, convinced that this is all —
verses.translation: Giving themselves over to immeasurable cares ending only with death, regarding gratification of lust as their highest aim, and feeling sure that that is all.
verses.explanation: The inner weather of the creed: cintām aparimeyām, anxiety without measure, scheduled to end pralayāntām — only at death. The logic is airtight: if enjoyment is everything and time is short, then worry is the only rational state. Etāvad iti niścitāḥ — 'this much is all there is,' the certainty that closes every window. The portrait's cruelest detail: they are not enjoying it.
verses.connection: The cost accounting begins: the pleasure-creed billed in lifelong, terminal anxiety.
verses.reflection: Measure the creed by its weather today: where enjoyment is everything, anxiety is the climate.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
आशापाशशतैर्बद्धाः कामक्रोधपरायणाः ।
ईहन्ते कामभोगार्थमन्यायेनार्थसञ्चयान्॥
verses.transliteration:
āśāpāśaśatairbaddhāḥ kāmakrodhaparāyaṇāḥ
īhante kāmabhogārthamanyāyenārthasañcayān
verses.essence: — bound by a hundred snares of hope, given over to desire and anger, they strive to amass wealth by wrongful means for the indulgence of desires.
verses.translation: Bound by a hundred ties of hope, given over to lust and anger, they strive to obtain by unlawful means hoards to wealth for sensual enjoyments.

anvaya.meaning: bound by a hundred nooses of hope

verses.explanation: Āśā-pāśa-śataiḥ: hope itself as a hundred nooses — the asuric life is tied not by despair but by *expectation*, each anticipated pleasure another cord. Desire and its bodyguard anger (the old pair from 3.37) run the operation; anyāyena, by whatever bends, the wealth piles up. The verse paints acquisition as a form of bondage wearing the costume of success.
verses.connection: The behavioral output: craving plus deadline equals accumulation without scruple.
verses.reflection: Count your nooses of hope tonight — the expectations binding tomorrow before it arrives. Cut one.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
इदमद्य मया लब्धमिमं प्राप्स्ये मनोरथम् ।
इदमस्तीदमपि मे भविष्यति पुनर्धनम्॥
verses.transliteration:
idamadya mayā labdhamimaṃ prāpsye manoratham
idamastīdamapi me bhaviṣyati punardhanam
verses.essence: 'This I have gained today; this longing I shall fulfill. This is mine — and this wealth too shall be mine.
verses.translation: "This has been gained by me today; this desire of mine I shall fuffil; this is mine and this wealth also shall be mine in future."

anvaya.meaning: 'this, today, has been gained by me
this is (mine); this also shall be

verses.explanation: The teaching changes register: for three verses (16.13–15) Krishna stops describing and starts *quoting* — the asuric inner monologue performed verbatim. Listen to the pronouns: idam, mayā, me, me — this, by me, mine, mine. A grammar with one person in it. The eerie power of the passage is its familiarity; everyone has caught this voice running in their own head at 2 a.m.
verses.connection: The soliloquy begins: the demonic rendered not as other, but as a voice the reader recognizes.
verses.reflection: Catch the monologue running — this is mine, this too shall be mine — and simply hear its grammar.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
असौ मया हतः शत्रुर्हनिष्ये चापरानपि ।
ईश्वरोऽहमहं भोगी सिद्धोऽहं बलवान्सुखी॥
verses.transliteration:
asau mayā hataḥ śatrurhaniṣye cāparānapi
īśvaro'hamahaṃ bhogī siddho'haṃ balavānsukhī
verses.essence: 'That enemy has been slain by me, and others too I shall slay. I am the lord, I am the enjoyer, I am accomplished, mighty and happy.
verses.translation: "That enemy has been slain by me; and others also I shall slay. I am the lord. I enjoy. I am perfect, powerful and happy."

anvaya.meaning: 'that enemy has been slain by me
and I shall slay others too
I am the lord; I am the enjoyer

verses.explanation: The monologue crescendos into theology: īśvaro 'ham — *I am the Lord* — the exact title this Gita reserves for the one speaking it now claimed by the deluded self. Every 'aham' is a small coronation: enjoyer, perfected, powerful, sukhī — happy, asserted rather than felt, the way one insists on what is slipping. The asura, finally, is not one who denies God but one who applies for the position.
verses.connection: The soliloquy's core: self-deification — the precise inversion of 15.18's Purushottama.
verses.reflection: When 'I am the lord' whispers in any costume today, smile and return the crown.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
आढ्योऽभिजनवानस्मि कोऽन्योऽस्ति सदृशो मया ।
यक्ष्ये दास्यामि मोदिष्य इत्यज्ञानविमोहिताः॥
verses.transliteration:
āḍhyo'bhijanavānasmi ko'nyo'sti sadṛśo mayā
yakṣye dāsyāmi modiṣya ityajñānavimohitāḥ
verses.essence: 'I am wealthy and well-born — who else is equal to me? I shall sacrifice, I shall give, I shall rejoice.' Thus deluded by unknowing —
verses.translation: "I am rich and born in a noble family. Who else is equal to me? I shall perform sacrifices. I shall give (charity). I shall rejoice," thus deluded by ignorance.

anvaya.meaning: who else is there like me?
I shall sacrifice, I shall give, I shall rejoice'

verses.explanation: Even religion enters the inventory: yakṣye dāsyāmi — I *shall* sacrifice, I *shall* donate — piety scheduled as another acquisition, worship as self-decoration. Ko 'nyo 'sti sadṛśo mayā: 'who is like me?' — the question that, asked of God, is awe, and asked of oneself, is the whole disease. The quotation closes on its diagnosis: ajñāna-vimohitāḥ, all of it spoken inside a fog.
verses.connection: The soliloquy ends with piety itself annexed by ego — and 16.16 shows where the fog leads.
verses.reflection: Watch piety serve the ego once today — the announced donation, the displayed virtue — first in yourself.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
अनेकचित्तविभ्रान्ता मोहजालसमावृताः ।
प्रसक्ताः कामभोगेषु पतन्ति नरकेऽशुचौ॥
verses.transliteration:
anekacittavibhrāntā mohajālasamāvṛtāḥ
prasaktāḥ kāmabhogeṣu patanti narake'śucau
verses.essence: — led astray by many fancies, wrapped in a net of delusion, addicted to the gratification of desires, they fall into a foul hell.
verses.translation: Bewildered by many a fancy, entangled in the snare of delusion, addicted to the gratification of lust, they fall into a foul hell.

anvaya.meaning: enveloped in a net of delusion
attached to the enjoyments of desire
they fall into an impure hell

verses.explanation: The trajectory completes: aneka-citta-vibhrāntāḥ, a mind scattered across its own schemes; moha-jāla, delusion as a *net* — each strand thin, the weave inescapable. The fall into naraka needs no external judge; a life built on 16.13–15's monologue is already constructing its own descent, the way a spiral stair needs no one to push.
verses.connection: The soliloquy's gravity: the net was self-woven, the fall self-propelled.
verses.reflection: Find one strand of the self-woven net today — a scheme, a fantasy — and decline to add another.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
आत्मसम्भाविताः स्तब्धा धनमानमदान्विताः ।
यजन्ते नामयज्ञैस्ते दम्भेनाविधिपूर्वकम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ātmasambhāvitāḥ stabdhā dhanamānamadānvitāḥ
yajante nāmayajñaiste dambhenāvidhipūrvakam
verses.essence: Self-honoring, stubborn, filled with the pride and intoxication of wealth, they perform sacrifices in name only, with hypocrisy, against all rule.
verses.translation: Self-conceited, stubborn, filled with the pride and intoxication of wealth, they perform sacrifices in name out of ostentation, contrary to scriptural ordinances.

anvaya.meaning: filled with wealth's pride and intoxication

verses.explanation: The religious performance examined: nāma-yajñaiḥ — sacrifices in name only, ritual as theater, the donation announced, the temple wing named after the donor. Ātma-sambhāvitāḥ: honored by themselves — having appointed themselves their own audience and judge, applause is guaranteed. Worship without surrender is the asuric creed's most refined costume.
verses.connection: Returns to 16.15's 'I shall sacrifice' and shows the performance up close.
verses.reflection: Do one good thing today with no audience at all — not even the inner one applauding.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
अहंकारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं च संश्रिताः ।
मामात्मपरदेहेषु प्रद्विषन्तोऽभ्यसूयकाः॥
verses.transliteration:
ahaṃkāraṃ balaṃ darpaṃ kāmaṃ krodhaṃ ca saṃśritāḥ
māmātmaparadeheṣu pradviṣanto'bhyasūyakāḥ
verses.essence: Clinging to ego, force, arrogance, desire and anger, these malicious ones hate me in their own bodies and in those of others.
verses.translation: Given over to egoism, power, haughtiness, lust and anger, these malicious people hate Me in their own bodies and in those of others.

anvaya.meaning: desire and anger — relying on these
me, in their own and others' bodies

verses.explanation: The portrait's theological depth-charge: mām ātma-para-deheṣu pradviṣantaḥ — they hate *me*, dwelling in their own bodies and in everyone else's. Every cruelty to another and every violence to oneself is, by chapter 15's indwelling doctrine, aimed at the same resident. No one has ever harmed a creature without the blow landing on God.
verses.connection: Connects the asuric life to 15.15's indweller: all hatred has, finally, one address.
verses.reflection: Remember the address of every cruelty today: the same resident, in your body and in theirs.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
तानहं द्विषतः क्रुरान्संसारेषु नराधमान् ।
क्षिपाम्यजस्रमशुभानासुरीष्वेव योनिषु॥
verses.transliteration:
tānahaṃ dviṣataḥ krurānsaṃsāreṣu narādhamān
kṣipāmyajasramaśubhānāsurīṣveva yoniṣu
verses.essence: These cruel haters, worst of men — I hurl them again and again into demonic wombs alone, in the cycles of birth and death.
verses.translation: Those cruel haters, worst among men in the world, I hurl those evil-doers into the wombs of demons only.

anvaya.meaning: in the rounds of samsara, lowest of men

verses.explanation: The hardest verse in the chapter, and tradition reads its verb with care: kṣipāmi, 'I hurl,' is the Lord speaking as the law of consequence itself — the same impartiality of 9.29 ('I am alike to all') seen from its stern face. Like gravitates to like: a consciousness drenched in cruelty is drawn to births where cruelty is the native climate. No caprice, no vendetta — trajectory, owned by the one who upholds all trajectories.
verses.connection: Consequence personified: 14.18's downward pull, spoken in the first person.
verses.schools_note: 'I hurl them into asuric wombs' — the verse that raises the question of damnation, and the tradition's answer is unanimous on one point: the Gita teaches no eternal hell. Shankara reads the hurling as karma's own machinery in the Lord's voice; Ramanuja as just consequence preserving the soul's freedom to turn; all cite 16.20's 'mam aprapya' — *not yet* attaining Me — as the open clause. Descent is long; the door is never welded.
verses.reflection: Read consequence as climate today, not vendetta: like settles toward like — so choose your like.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
आसुरीं योनिमापन्ना मूढा जन्मनि जन्मनि ।
मामप्राप्यैव कौन्तेय ततो यान्त्यधमां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
āsurīṃ yonimāpannā mūḍhā janmani janmani
māmaprāpyaiva kaunteya tato yāntyadhamāṃ gatim
verses.essence: Entering demonic wombs, deluded birth after birth, failing to reach me, Kaunteya, they sink to the lowest state.
verses.translation: Entering into demoniacal wombs and deluded, birth after birth, not attaining Me, they thus fall, O Arjuna, into a condition still lower than that.

anvaya.meaning: not attaining me at all, O Kaunteya
thence they go to the lowest state

verses.explanation: The descent's full arc — and its hidden hinge: mām aprāpya *eva*, 'not yet reaching me.' Commentators across schools fasten on that word: the door is unclosed; the descent is long, not eternal. The Gita knows no everlasting damnation — only trajectories, and trajectories can be turned. Even from the lowest rung, the ladder of 12.8–11 still reaches.
verses.connection: Bottom of the descent — with the exit deliberately left unbarred, as 16.21–22 confirms.
verses.reflection: Hold the unbarred door for someone written off — including the written-off corner of yourself.
verses.background: The asuric portrait is drawn at full length, unflinching: they know neither action nor renunciation, neither purity nor truth; they hold the world baseless, godless, born of lust; with insatiable desires, bound by a hundred nooses of hope, given to hypocritical sacrifice and self-glorification — 'this I have gained, this too shall be mine; I am the lord, the enjoyer' — hating Me in their own and others' bodies, they fall, birth after birth, into ever lower wombs, not attaining Me.

verses.devanagari:
त्रिविधं नरकस्येदं द्वारं नाशनमात्मनः ।
कामः क्रोधस्तथा लोभस्तस्मादेतत्त्रयं त्यजेत्॥
verses.transliteration:
trividhaṃ narakasyedaṃ dvāraṃ nāśanamātmanaḥ
kāmaḥ krodhastathā lobhastasmādetattrayaṃ tyajet
verses.essence: Threefold is this gate of hell, destructive of the self: desire, anger, and greed. Therefore let one abandon these three.
verses.translation: Triple is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self lust, anger and greed; therefore one should abandon these three.

anvaya.meaning: the gate, destructive of the self
therefore let one abandon this triad

verses.explanation: After fourteen verses of portrait, the practical distillation: hell has exactly three doors — kāma, krodha, lobha — and they open from the inside. The entire demonic catastrophe, traced back, passes through one of these hinges. The instruction is correspondingly finite: tyajet, drop these three, and the whole edifice of 16.7–20 loses its foundation. Diagnosis becomes doorkeeping. Tri-vidham narakasya dvaram — threefold is hell's gate: desire, anger, greed — the verse every Indian schoolchild once memorized as moral arithmetic. The commentaries stress the architecture: hell here has doors, not walls — entry is by passage, not by sentence, and the doors open from the inside. Tasmad etat trayam tyajet: the entire demonic catastrophe of the chapter, traced back, passes through three hinges a person can guard.
verses.connection: The chapter's actionable core: the vast darkness reduced to three guarded doors.
verses.reflection: Guard the three doors today by name: one craving declined, one anger cooled, one grasping opened.
verses.background: The chapter closes with diagnosis and compass: threefold is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self — desire, anger, greed; therefore abandon these three. The one released from these three gates of darkness practices what is good and reaches the supreme goal. So let scripture be your measure in what is to be done and not done — knowing the scriptural rule, perform your work here.

verses.devanagari:
एतैर्विमुक्तः कौन्तेय तमोद्वारैस्त्रिभिर्नरः ।
आचरत्यात्मनः श्रेयस्ततो याति परां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
etairvimuktaḥ kaunteya tamodvāraistribhirnaraḥ
ācaratyātmanaḥ śreyastato yāti parāṃ gatim
verses.essence: One who is released from these three gates of darkness, Kaunteya, practices what is good for the self — and so reaches the supreme goal.
verses.translation: A man who is liberated from these three gates to darkness, O Arjuna, practises what is good for him and thus goes to the Supreme Goal.

anvaya.meaning: the three gates of darkness, a person
practices the good of the self
thence goes to the supreme goal

verses.explanation: The positive corollary, with a quiet insight tucked inside: ācaraty ātmanaḥ śreyaḥ — only *after* release from the three does one even begin practicing one's own true good. Desire, anger and greed do not merely cause suffering; they impersonate the self's interests so well that genuine self-care is impossible while they govern. Freedom from them is where real life starts.
verses.connection: Mirror of 16.21: the three doors closed, the road of the whole Gita reopens.
verses.reflection: Notice what genuine self-care becomes possible the hour the three doormen are off duty.
verses.background: The chapter closes with diagnosis and compass: threefold is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self — desire, anger, greed; therefore abandon these three. The one released from these three gates of darkness practices what is good and reaches the supreme goal. So let scripture be your measure in what is to be done and not done — knowing the scriptural rule, perform your work here.

verses.devanagari:
यः शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य वर्तते कामकारतः ।
न स सिद्धिमवाप्नोति न सुखं न परां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yaḥ śāstravidhimutsṛjya vartate kāmakārataḥ
na sa siddhimavāpnoti na sukhaṃ na parāṃ gatim
verses.essence: One who casts aside the ordinances of scripture and acts by the impulse of desire attains neither perfection, nor happiness, nor the supreme goal.
verses.translation: He who, having cast aside the ordinances of the scriptures, acts under the impulse of desire, attains not perfection, nor happiness nor the Supreme Goal.

anvaya.meaning: who, casting aside the scripture's rule
acts from the impulse of desire
nor happiness, nor the supreme goal

verses.explanation: The closing argument: why śāstra at all? Because kāma-kārataḥ — 'desire-driven' — is the only alternative compass, and the chapter has just spent twenty verses showing where it navigates. Scripture here is not arbitrary command but accumulated sight: the recorded vision of those who saw the road's whole length. Discarding the map, one steers by appetite — and appetite, 16.10 said, has no destination at all.
verses.connection: Grounds the chapter's ethics: without a measure beyond desire, 16.13's monologue becomes the only voice.
verses.reflection: Ask of one impulse today: is this guided by anything beyond appetite? If not, consult the map.
verses.background: The chapter closes with diagnosis and compass: threefold is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self — desire, anger, greed; therefore abandon these three. The one released from these three gates of darkness practices what is good and reaches the supreme goal. So let scripture be your measure in what is to be done and not done — knowing the scriptural rule, perform your work here.

verses.devanagari:
तस्माच्छास्त्रं प्रमाणं ते कार्याकार्यव्यवस्थितौ ।
ज्ञात्वा शास्त्रविधानोक्तं कर्म कर्तुमिहार्हसि॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmācchāstraṃ pramāṇaṃ te kāryākāryavyavasthitau
jñātvā śāstravidhānoktaṃ karma kartumihārhasi
verses.essence: Therefore let scripture be your measure in determining what is to be done and what is not. Knowing what the scriptural rule declares, you should act in this world.
verses.translation: Therefore, let the scripture be thy authority in determining what ought to be done and what ought not to be done. Having known what is said in the ordinance of the scriptures, thou shouldst act here in this world.

anvaya.meaning: in settling the to-be-done and not-to-be-done

verses.explanation: The chapter ends not in fear but in orientation: pramāṇam, a *measure* — desire measures everything by appetite; the asura by self; the seeker needs a standard outside both. Jñātvā... kartum arhasi: know, *then* act — scripture as informed compass, not blind cage; the Gita itself will soon (18.63) tell Arjuna to deliberate and choose freely. Between the two ledgers of this chapter, a measuring-line is placed in the listener's hand. Tasmach chhastram pramanam te — therefore let scripture be your measure: the chapter's closing compass, and the commentaries are careful with its force. Pramana means an instrument of valid knowing, not a cage: the verse installs a standard outside appetite, because the alternative compass (kama-karatah, desire-driven) has just been shown navigating to ruin. Jnatva... kartum arhasi — know, then act: informed conduct, not blind compliance; the Gita itself will end by saying 'deliberate, then do as you choose.'
verses.connection: Closes the two-natures teaching with a compass — and opens ch17's question: what of those who act in faith beyond the rules?
verses.reflection: Keep a measure outside your moods today — let something steadier than desire settle one decision.
verses.background: The chapter closes with diagnosis and compass: threefold is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self — desire, anger, greed; therefore abandon these three. The one released from these three gates of darkness practices what is good and reaches the supreme goal. So let scripture be your measure in what is to be done and not done — knowing the scriptural rule, perform your work here.
